lessons.title: Sorting an array using selection sort

exercises.title: Selection Sort on Integer Array
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Implement selection sort to sort an array of integers in **ascending** order and print the result.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (the number of elements, 1 <= n <= 20)
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers

## Output

- A single line containing the sorted integers, each separated by a space, with a trailing space after the last element.

## Requirements

- Use a C-style array or `std::array` to store the elements.
- Implement selection sort yourself with nested loops — do not use any library sorting function.
- Use `std::swap` from `<utility>` to swap elements.

## Notes

- The outer loop should iterate from the first element to the second-to-last element.
- The inner loop finds the index of the minimum value in the unsorted portion.

exercises.title: Selection Sort a Vector of Structs by Field
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Read a list of students (name and score), then use selection sort to sort them by score in **descending** order (highest score first). Print the sorted list.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (number of students, 1 <= n <= 15)
- Next `n` lines: a string (name, no spaces) followed by an integer (score)

## Output

- `n` lines, each containing the name and score separated by a space, sorted from highest to lowest score.

## Requirements

- Define a `struct Student` with `std::string name` and `int score` members.
- Store the students in a `std::vector<Student>`.
- Implement selection sort yourself — do not use any library sorting function.
- When comparing, sort by score descending. If two students have the same score, their relative order does not matter (any order is accepted).

## Notes

- This is the same selection sort algorithm from the lesson, but you are sorting structs by a specific field and in descending order.
- To sort descending, find the **maximum** in the unsorted region instead of the minimum.

lessons.title: Introduction to iterators

exercises.title: Iterator Traversal Printer
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Read integers into a `std::vector`, then use an explicit iterator loop to print each element and compute the sum.

## Input
- Line 1: a single integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20), the number of elements.
- Line 2: exactly `N` space-separated integers.

## Output
- Line 1: `Elements:` followed by a space, then all elements separated by spaces, with a trailing newline (no trailing space).
- Line 2: `Sum: S` where S is the sum of all elements.

## Requirements
1. Declare a `std::vector<int>` and read `N` values into it using `push_back`.
2. Use an explicit iterator loop (`auto it = vec.begin(); it != vec.end(); ++it`) to traverse the vector.
3. Inside the loop, dereference the iterator with `*it` to access each element.
4. Print elements separated by single spaces with no trailing space. Hint: print a space before every element except the first.
5. Accumulate the sum using `*it` during the same loop.
6. Include `<vector>` and `<iostream>`.

exercises.title: Iterator-Based Maximum Finder
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Read integers into a `std::array`, then use iterators to find and report the maximum value and its position.

## Input
- A single line containing exactly 6 space-separated integers.

## Output
- Line 1: `Max: M` where M is the largest element in the array.
- Line 2: `Position: P` where P is the zero-based index of the first occurrence of the maximum.

## Requirements
1. Declare a `std::array<int, 6>` and read all 6 values from stdin.
2. Initialize a `max` variable by dereferencing `arr.begin()`.
3. Initialize a `position` variable to `0`.
4. Use an explicit iterator loop starting from `arr.begin()` to traverse the array.
5. Inside the loop, compare `*it` against `max`. If it is strictly greater, update `max` and compute the position as the distance from `arr.begin()` to `it` (subtract iterators: `static_cast<int>(it - arr.begin())`).
6. Do not use any standard library algorithm. Do not use index-based access to find the maximum.
7. Include `<array>` and `<iostream>`.

test_cases.label: Hidden -- duplicate max keeps first position

lessons.title: Introduction to standard library algorithms

exercises.title: Sort, Search, and Summarize
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Read a list of integers, sort them, search for a target value, and print summary statistics using standard library algorithms.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 20) -- the number of elements.
- Line 2: `N` integers separated by spaces.
- Line 3: a single integer `T` -- the target value to search for.

## Output
Exactly four lines:
1. `Sorted:` followed by a space, then all elements in ascending order separated by spaces.
2. `Min: <value>` -- the smallest element.
3. `Sum: <value>` -- the sum of all elements.
4. `Found <T>: yes` if `T` exists in the array, or `Found <T>: no` if it does not.

Example for input `5`, `3 1 4 1 5`, `4`:
```
Sorted: 1 1 3 4 5
Min: 1
Sum: 14
Found 4: yes
```

## Requirements
1. Store the elements in a `std::vector<int>`.
2. Use `std::sort` to sort the vector.
3. Use `std::min_element` to find the minimum value.
4. Use `std::accumulate` (from `<numeric>`) to compute the sum.
5. Use `std::find` to search for the target. Compare the returned iterator against `end()` to determine if the target was found.
6. Do **not** use lambdas.

exercises.title: Count and Extremes Report
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Read a list of integers and produce a report using `std::count`, `std::max_element`, `std::for_each`, and `std::accumulate`.

## Input
- Line 1: two integers `N` (1 <= N <= 20) and `Q` separated by a space -- `N` is the element count and `Q` is the value to count.
- Line 2: `N` integers separated by spaces.

## Output
Exactly three lines:
1. `Max: <value>` -- the largest element in the list.
2. `Count of <Q>: <c>` -- how many times `Q` appears.
3. `Double sum: <value>` -- the sum of every element after doubling. Use `std::for_each` with a named function to double each element in place, then use `std::accumulate` to compute the sum.

Example for input `6 3` and `3 1 3 2 3 4`:
```
Max: 4
Count of 3: 3
Double sum: 32
```
(Elements doubled: 6 2 6 4 6 8 -> sum = 32)

## Requirements
1. Store the elements in a `std::vector<int>`.
2. Use `std::max_element` (dereference the result) to find the maximum.
3. Use `std::count` to count occurrences of `Q`.
4. Write a named function `void doubleValue(int& x)` that multiplies `x` by 2.
5. Use `std::for_each` with `doubleValue` to double every element in the vector.
6. Use `std::accumulate` to sum the doubled elements.
7. Do **not** use lambdas.

lessons.title: Timing your code

exercises.title: Duration Conversion Calculator
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Write a program that reads a number of microseconds and converts it to milliseconds and seconds using `std::chrono` duration types.

## Input
- A single integer on one line representing a count of microseconds.

## Output
Exactly three lines:
```
<N> us
<M> ms
<S> s
```
Where `<N>` is the original microsecond value, `<M>` is the truncated millisecond value, and `<S>` is the truncated seconds value. All values are integers.

## Requirements
1. Read the integer input into a `std::chrono::microseconds` duration using its constructor.
2. Use `std::chrono::duration_cast` to convert to `std::chrono::milliseconds` and `std::chrono::seconds`.
3. Use `.count()` to extract the numeric value from each duration for printing.
4. The conversions truncate toward zero (this is the default behavior of `duration_cast`).
5. You may use `#include <chrono>` and `#include <iostream>`.

exercises.title: Time Difference from Timestamps
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Simulate computing elapsed time between two timestamps. Read two integer values representing "start" and "end" times in milliseconds, compute the difference using `std::chrono` duration arithmetic, and report the result in multiple units.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer representing the start time in milliseconds.
- Line 2: an integer representing the end time in milliseconds. The end time is always >= the start time.

## Output
Exactly three lines:
```
Elapsed: <D> ms
Elapsed: <U> us
Elapsed: <S> s
```
Where `<D>` is the difference in milliseconds, `<U>` is that difference converted to microseconds, and `<S>` is the difference truncated to whole seconds.

## Requirements
1. Read both integers and construct `std::chrono::milliseconds` durations from each.
2. Compute the elapsed duration by subtracting the start duration from the end duration (duration arithmetic).
3. Convert the elapsed duration to `std::chrono::microseconds` -- this conversion is implicit (finer precision, no cast needed).
4. Convert the elapsed duration to `std::chrono::seconds` using `std::chrono::duration_cast` (coarser precision requires explicit cast).
5. Print all three results using `.count()` in the exact format shown.
6. You may use `#include <chrono>` and `#include <iostream>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- 1000 to 4500 ms
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Elapsed: 3500 ms
Elapsed: 3500000 us
Elapsed: 3 s

test_cases.label: Hidden -- 0 to 750 ms (less than 1 second)
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Elapsed: 750 ms
Elapsed: 750000 us
Elapsed: 0 s

test_cases.label: Hidden -- same start and end
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Elapsed: 0 ms
Elapsed: 0 us
Elapsed: 0 s
